title: A Reidemeister-Singer Conjecture for Surface Diagrams
speaker: Jonathan Williams
abstract: There is a way to specify any smooth, closed oriented four-manifold using a surface decorated with simple closed curves, something I call a surface diagram. In this talk I will describe three moves on these objects, two of which are reminiscent of Heegaard diagrams for three-manifolds. These may form part of a uniqueness theorem for such diagrams that is likely to be useful for understanding Floer theories for non-symplectic four-manifolds.

proof_stages.label: Near-symplectic Gromov-invariant motivation
proof_stages.summary: The talk motivates surface-diagram uniqueness by the program of viewing Seiberg-Witten invariants as Gromov-type counts of pseudo-holomorphic multisections for near-symplectic four-manifolds.

proof_stages.label: Broken/wrinkled fibration local apparatus
proof_stages.summary: Broken Lefschetz or purely wrinkled fibrations are described via Lefschetz points, indefinite folds, cusps, and the known map-level moves—birth, merge, flip, and isotopy—that connect homotopic such maps.

proof_stages.label: Reduction to surface diagrams
proof_stages.summary: The general complicated critical-value diagram is simplified to a single embedded cusped fold circle, and for sufficiently high genus the higher-genus fiber together with its cyclic vanishing cycles determines the four-manifold, giving the notion of a surface diagram.

proof_stages.label: Need for diagram-level Reidemeister-Singer moves
proof_stages.summary: Because invariance of multisection counts is hard for arbitrary map-level moves, the speaker isolates the desired uniqueness problem: describe moves directly on surface diagrams that suffice for homotopic maps.

proof_stages.label: Surface-diagram stabilization
proof_stages.summary: A stabilization increases the fiber genus near a chosen vanishing cycle and inserts a controlled block of new vanishing cycles, and it is realized by flip moves so it preserves the underlying homotopy class of the fibration map.

proof_stages.label: Surface-diagram handle slide
proof_stages.summary: When three consecutive vanishing cycles are disjoint from a separated fourth one, a vanishing cycle may be slid over the fourth; geometrically this comes from pushing a fold arc through a lower-genus region and pulling it back.

proof_stages.label: Multislide from merge and inverse merge
proof_stages.summary: Since stabilization and handle slide preserve the homotopy class of the critical circle, a multislide is introduced by merging two fold arcs and unmerging along a different arc, thereby changing the critical-loop homotopy class and inducing a more complicated transformation of vanishing cycles.

proof_stages.label: Reidemeister-Singer statement and connected-fiber payoff
proof_stages.summary: The resulting theorem/conjecture says that homotopic surface diagrams of a simply connected four-manifold are related by stabilizations and handle slides, while in the non-simply-connected case multislides are also needed; these moves have the desirable feature that fibers remain connected throughout.

examples.name: Donaldson-Smith standard surface count
examples.note: The symplectic Lefschetz-fibration case, where Donaldson-Smith's multisection count agrees with Seiberg-Witten by Usher, illustrates that the multisection-count strategy can work before the talk turns to near-symplectic diagrams.

examples.name: Low-genus gluing ambiguity
examples.note: The genus-two and sphere lower-fiber cases illustrate why the genus-at-least-three hypothesis removes extra choices in closing off the simplified fibration.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker analyzes the low-genus cases by relating genus two to a torus downstairs and explaining the resulting multiple gluing choices via the fundamental group of the diffeomorphism group

passes.reason: The low-genus boundary cases have less topology and nonunique gluing, showing the genus-at-least-three uniqueness claim would fail there.

examples.name: Bad kink pinching off a null-homologous sphere
examples.note: A backward local deformation producing two parallel vanishing cycles and a disconnected fiber illustrates why the preferred moves, which keep fibers connected, are useful for Lagrangian matching considerations.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker traces the bad kink deformation through two parallel vanishing-cycle crossings and derives that the fiber contains two disks with a null-homologous sphere pinched off

passes.reason: The kink creates a disconnected fiber with a null-homologous sphere component, obstructing the Lagrangian matching invariant.
